text: The surgeon prepares for a total laparoscopic hysterectomy on a bulky uterus by placing a supraumbilical port. A small stab incision is made, and the abdominal wall is lifted to introduce the Veress needle. After confirming the needle's placement with irrigation, suction, and the hanging drop test, CO₂ insufflation begins at a rate of one liter per minute, with the insufflator preset pressure set to fifteen millimeters of mercury. Once the pressure stabilizes, the Veress needle is removed, the incision is enlarged to eleven millimeters, and the optical port is introduced.

The surgeon connects the gas for insufflation and applies the baseball diamond concept for port placement. Two trocars are inserted 7.5 cm lateral to the midline for ipsilateral ports, with an additional contralateral port for the assistant. A uterine manipulator is introduced after dilating the cervix with a #5 Hegar dilator, using a Valsalva maneuver to hold the cervix. A sound is introduced for further cervical dilation, assisted by a speculum.

The surgeon uses a number five Hegar dilator followed by the Mangeshkar uterine manipulator with a 3.5 cm colpotomizer. Contralateral traction is applied, and the round ligament is coagulated. The patient has a right wall fibroid and multiple intramural fibroids, contributing to the bulky uterus. The surgeon coagulates the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovarian ligament, then dissects 2.5 cm over the broad ligament using a ligature. The right adnexa is separated, and the same process is repeated on the left side, with the sigmoid colon visible below the ovarian ligament.

The surgeon retroverts the uterus and opens the anterior leaf of the broad ligament using harmonic energy to create an areolar tissue plane. The assistant rotates the uterus from the five o'clock to seven o'clock position. The UV fold is opened, and the bladder is lifted as the uterus is pushed towards the seven o'clock position. The surgeon dissects the right side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, exposing the uterine artery. The uterus is positioned at six o'clock, and the bladder is separated using blunt dissection, revealing the shiny vaginal fascia. The surgeon continues lateral dissection towards the bladder pillar.

The surgeon opens the posterior peritoneum, separating it up to the arc of the uterosacral ligament due to the presence of a large fibroid in the posterior wall. The uterus is lifted with a grasper, and the light cable of the 30-degree telescope is positioned at six o'clock to visualize the posterior cul-de-sac. The uterine artery is coagulated without cutting initially, and the right side of the posterior peritoneum is separated using a ligature. The uterine artery on the right side is coagulated and cut, followed by division of the uterine tissue. The procedure is repeated on the left side, where the uterine tissue is also divided.

The surgeon begins the colpotomy using the tip of the colpotomizer, starting with the left lateral and anterior sections. The harmonic scalpel is used carefully to avoid damaging the colpotomizer. The anterior colpotomy is extended to the right side, followed by the right side colpotomy using the harmonic scalpel through the right port. The posterior colpotomy is performed, with half completed from the right side and the remaining half from the left side.

The surgeon pulls the uterus into the vagina and bisects it for removal due to its large size. Using a number one Vicryl suture, the surgeon takes bites on the uterosacral ligament and posterior vaginal vault, ensuring the bladder is pushed behind to avoid pricking. Suturing continues from right to left, with full thickness of the fascia taken, requiring six bites in total. The left corner is sutured, involving the uterosacral ligament and anterior fornix, with interlocking sutures applied for closure.

The surgeon interlocks the sutures and uses a surgeon's knot to complete the valve closure. The suture tail is pulled through a 5mm port, and the cannula is removed to extract the needle. Suction irrigation is performed to check for bleeding, and fluid is aspirated from the posterior cul-de-sac, confirming no bleeding.
